questions.stem: A 19-year-old university student presents with a 5-day history of fever, severe sore throat, and fatigue. On examination, temperature is 38.5°C, there are bilateral tender cervical lymph nodes, tonsillar exudates, and palatal petechiae. The spleen is not palpable. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Palatal petechiae are classic for infectious mononucleosis (EBV). Streptococcal tonsillitis does not typically cause palatal petechiae or posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Diphtheria presents with a grey pseudomembrane and is rare in the UK. HIV seroconversion can mimic mononucleosis but rash and oral ulcers are more common. CMV mononucleosis tends to have less pharyngitis.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of fever, malaise, and myalgia. She has no sore throat. Examination reveals mild cervical lymphadenopathy and mild hepatomegaly. Investigations show lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes, ALT 120 U/L, AST 90 U/L, and negative Monospot test. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In older adults, EBV can present without pharyngitis. Atypical lymphocytosis and transaminitis are typical. CMV usually has a similar presentation but is an important mimic; however, EBV is more common. Toxoplasmosis often has posterior cervical nodes. Lymphoma would not have acute fever and atypical reactive lymphocytes. Drug history is negative.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old man with infectious mononucleosis diagnosed 1 week ago presents with sudden severe left upper quadrant pain radiating to the left shoulder, and dizziness. On examination, he is pale, heart rate 110 bpm, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg. Abdomen is tender in the left upper quadrant. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Splenic rupture is a well-known complication of infectious mononucleosis. Left shoulder tip pain (Kehr's sign) and hypotension indicate ruptured spleen. Pancreatitis is less likely without alcohol or gallstones. Perforated ulcer would have epigastric pain and peritonism. Infarction is rare. Abscess would have fever, not acute collapse.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with fever, jaundice, and tender hepatomegaly. He also reports mild sore throat and cervical lymphadenopathy. Investigations show bilirubin 40 µmol/L, ALT 250 U/L, ALP normal. Peripheral blood shows lymphocytes with atypical forms. Monospot test is negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: EBV hepatitis is often part of infectious mononucleosis; the sore throat and lymphadenopathy point to EBV. Monospot can be false-negative early. Hepatitis A/B usually have no pharyngitis. CMV hepatitis is similar but less common. Autoimmune hepatitis is chronic or relapsing, not acute with fever and atypical lymphocytes.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old girl presents with fever, severe headache, photophobia, and nuchal rigidity. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, raised protein. She also has mild cervical lymphadenopathy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: EBV can cause aseptic meningitis as part of primary infection; cervical lymphadenopathy is a clue. Enterovirus meningitis is usually in summer and lacks lymphadenopathy. TB meningitis has subacute course and low CSF glucose. Partially treated bacterial meningitis would have neutrophils. Lyme disease may have erythema migrans and is not typically associated with fever and aseptic meningitis without other features.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old university student presents with a 5-day history of sore throat, fever, and bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy. On examination, she has pharyngeal erythema and palpable tender lymph nodes. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Throat swab for group A streptococcus
questions.explanation: In a young adult with classic symptoms of infectious mononucleosis, the Monospot test is the recommended first-line investigation because it is rapid, specific for EBV, and widely available. Full blood count may show lymphocytosis but is not confirmatory.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man presents with a 2-week history of fever and generalised lymphadenopathy. He has no sore throat. Monospot test is negative. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
questions.explanation: In a patient with suspected mononucleosis but negative Monospot, EBV serology (VCA IgM) should be checked as heterophile antibodies can be absent early in the illness or in atypical presentations. CMV and toxoplasma are differentials but not the immediate next step.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with fatigue and mild pharyngitis has a blood film showing 70% lymphocytes with many atypical cells. Monospot is positive. Which serological pattern confirms acute primary EBV infection?
options: VCA IgM positive, VCA IgG positive, EBNA IgG negative
VCA IgM negative, VCA IgG positive, EBNA IgG positive
VCA IgM positive, VCA IgG negative, EBNA IgG negative
VCA IgM positive, VCA IgG positive, EBNA IgG positive
VCA IgM negative, VCA IgG negative, EBNA IgG negative
questions.explanation: Acute primary EBV infection is characterised by VCA IgM positivity without VCA IgG (or low avidity) and absence of EBNA IgG (which appears later in convalescence). The pattern in option 1 is diagnostic of acute infection.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with fever and lymphadenopathy has a negative Monospot. EBV serology shows: VCA IgM negative, VCA IgG positive (high titre), EBNA IgG positive. What is the most likely interpretation?
questions.explanation: A pattern of VCA IgM negative, VCA IgG positive, and EBNA IgG positive indicates past (remote) EBV infection with immunity. In acute infection, VCA IgM would be positive and EBNA IgG negative. As the Monospot is negative, other causes of fever and lymphadenopathy should be considered.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old university student presents with sore throat, fever, and lymphadenopathy. Monospot test is positive. She has no stridor, no dehydration. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral prednisolone 40 mg daily for 7 days
Oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily
Intravenous aciclovir 10 mg/kg three times daily
Symptomatic management with rest, hydration, and paracetamol
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend no specific antiviral therapy for uncomplicated infectious mononucleosis; symptomatic care is first-line. Amoxicillin commonly causes a morbilliform rash in EBV infection and should be avoided. Corticosteroids are reserved for severe complications such as airway obstruction or haemolytic anaemia.

questions.stem: A 21-year-old with confirmed infectious mononucleosis develops progressive stridor and difficulty swallowing. Examination shows massive tonsillar hypertrophy with near-complete airway obstruction. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids (e.g., IV dexamethasone) are the first-line treatment for severe upper airway obstruction due to tonsillar hypertrophy in infectious mononucleosis. Antivirals are not beneficial. Antibiotics are indicated only if bacterial superinfection is confirmed. Tracheostomy is reserved for failure of medical therapy.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old with infectious mononucleosis presents with fatigue, pallor, and jaundice. Hb 70 g/L, reticulocytosis, positive direct Coombs test (IgG). What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Blood transfusion of packed red cells
questions.explanation: Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia secondary to EBV is typically treated with corticosteroids (prednisolone) as first-line. Splenectomy and rituximab are reserved for refractory cases. Blood transfusion may exacerbate haemolysis and is avoided unless life-threatening. Antivirals are not effective.

questions.stem: A 28-week pregnant woman presents with sore throat, fever, and cervical lymphadenopathy. Monospot test is positive. She is otherwise well. Which of the following drugs should be avoided because it commonly causes a rash in this condition?
questions.explanation: Amoxicillin-containing antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav) are notorious for causing a non-allergic maculopapular rash in patients with infectious mononucleosis, occurring in up to 90% of cases. Paracetamol, ibuprofen, aciclovir, and prednisolone are not associated with this specific rash and are safe to use if indicated.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old renal transplant recipient on tacrolimus and mycophenolate mofetil presents with fever, lymphadenopathy, and a positive EBV PCR (10,000 copies/mL). What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Increase immunosuppression with higher tacrolimus dose
questions.explanation: In solid organ transplant recipients with EBV disease, reduction of immunosuppression is the cornerstone of management to allow host immune control. Antivirals such as valganciclovir may be used adjunctively. Increasing immunosuppression would worsen the infection. Amoxicillin is not indicated, and corticosteroids are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old university student presents with a 5-day history of sore throat, fever, and fatigue. On examination, there is bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, tonsillar exudate, and no stridor. A Monospot test is positive. He asks when he can return to playing rugby. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Advise rest and avoid contact sports for 4 weeks
Prescribe aciclovir 800 mg five times daily
Prescribe amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily
Refer urgently to ear, nose and throat (ENT)
questions.explanation: The patient has glandular fever (infectious mononucleosis) with no airway compromise. Amoxicillin is contraindicated due to the high risk of morbilliform rash. Aciclovir is not recommended as it does not alter the disease course. Symptoms are typical for EBV, so CMV serology is not immediately required. The key immediate action is to advise avoidance of contact sports for 4 weeks to reduce the risk of splenic rupture.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman presents with fever, sore throat, and worsening dysphagia. On examination, she has marked tonsillar hypertrophy with bilateral exudate, stridor at rest, and drooling. A Monospot test is positive. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Request urgent CT neck with contrast
Administer high-flow oxygen via a rebreather mask
questions.explanation: This patient has severe tonsillar hypertrophy causing impending airway obstruction in the setting of infectious mononucleosis. The immediate first step is to give intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., dexamethasone) to reduce oedema. Oxygen can be given simultaneously but steroids treat the cause. Urgent ENT referral is needed, but the doctor on scene should give steroids first. Antibiotics are not indicated (and may cause rash). Tonsillectomy is not first-line and carries risks of bleeding.

questions.stem: A 21-year-old male who was diagnosed with infectious mononucleosis 10 days ago presents to the Emergency Department with sudden severe left upper quadrant pain after a minor collision during a game of football. He is tachycardic and hypotensive. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give oral analgesia and discharge with advice to avoid contact sports
Request an urgent CT abdomen with intravenous contrast
Obtain a surgical opinion and cross-match blood
Perform an urgent ultrasound of the abdomen
questions.explanation: This presentation is highly suspicious for traumatic splenic rupture, a life-threatening complication of infectious mononucleosis. The most appropriate immediate next step is to obtain a CT scan (contrast-enhanced) to confirm the diagnosis. Ultrasound may be less sensitive for active bleeding. Surgery (splenectomy) is not the first step; diagnosis must be confirmed. Cross-matching blood is important but should not delay imaging. Analgesia and discharge are inappropriate given haemodynamic instability.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with confirmed infectious mononucleosis develops jaundice. Observations are normal and she is clinically well. Blood tests show: bilirubin 50 µmol/L, ALT 300 U/L, ALP 120 U/L, INR 1.2. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer for urgent liver transplant assessment
Check EBV serology to confirm the diagnosis
Start oral prednisolone 40 mg once daily
Monitor LFTs and INR as an outpatient with GP follow-up
questions.explanation: Hepatitis in infectious mononucleosis is usually mild and self-limiting. The low INR suggests no significant synthetic dysfunction, so the patient can be safely managed as an outpatient with monitoring. EBV serology is already positive (diagnosis confirmed). Steroids are not indicated for uncomplicated hepatitis. N-acetylcysteine is used for paracetamol overdose, not EBV. Transplant is not required for mild derangement.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman (12 weeks gestation) presents with fever, sore throat, and cervical lymphadenopathy. A Monospot test is positive. She is anxious about effects on the baby. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start oral prednisolone to prevent fetal infection
Reassure her that EBV poses low risk to the fetus
questions.explanation: Infectious mononucleosis in pregnancy is generally not associated with congenital abnormalities or serious fetal harm; the primary risk is to the mother. Immediate management is reassurance, as the most appropriate next step is to address the patient's anxiety with accurate information. Antivirals (valaciclovir) are not recommended. Routine referral or fetal ultrasound is not indicated unless complications arise. Steroids are not used for fetal protection.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old student presents with sore throat, fever, and swollen lymph nodes. She has bilateral tonsillar exudates and palatal petechiae. Which single finding would raise immediate concern for a life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Splenic rupture is a rare but life-threatening complication of EBV infection. Left upper quadrant pain, especially when spontaneous or after minor trauma, suggests rupture, whereas splenomegaly alone is common but not an emergency.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old male with confirmed glandular fever is on the ward. He has a fever and cervical lymphadenopathy. On day 4 he develops progressive difficulty opening his mouth. Which finding is the red flag for an acute emergency?
questions.explanation: Trismus (impaired mouth opening) may indicate peritonsillar abscess or impending airway obstruction due to tonsillar enlargement, an immediate airway threat. Other options are common but not red flags.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with EBV infectious mononucleosis presents to ED with sudden severe epigastric pain, shoulder tip pain, and lightheadedness. Her blood pressure is 85/55 mmHg. Which of the following is the most likely life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Splenic rupture is a well-known complication of EBV. The combination of sudden left upper quadrant/epigastric pain, referred shoulder tip pain (Kehr's sign), and hypotension in a young patient with splenomegaly makes rupture the most likely life-threatening event.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old male presents with sore throat, fever, and cervical lymphadenopathy. He has never had these symptoms before. His blood tests show lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes. Which single finding would make you suspect a different diagnosis that is a medical emergency?
questions.explanation: Hilar lymphadenopathy is not typical of EBV; it raises suspicion for lymphoma or sarcoidosis, which are urgent diagnoses. In contrast, cervical lymphadenopathy, palatal petechiae, exudative tonsillitis, and periorbital oedema are classic for EBV.

questions.stem: A 21-year-old woman with serologically confirmed infectious mononucleosis returns to clinic with a new complaint. She now has difficulty walking and ascending stairs. On examination, she has symmetrical proximal muscle weakness and loss of deep tendon reflexes. Which of the following complications should be suspected?
questions.explanation: EBV can trigger Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS), presenting with ascending flaccid paralysis and areflexia. Encephalitis would cause altered mental status; myocarditis involves chest pain/arrhythmia; polymyositis often has muscle tenderness and not areflexia; ADEM has multifocal neurological signs.
